replies.author: @sicilianr1
replies.text:
@Cwin-ny6bp this isn’t a secret, logging and fishing are always the most dangerous jobs.   Soldiers don’t even make the top 10 per capita.  Far more are killed in the trades on a yearly basis.   

Statistical data indicates that in the United States, certain trades, particularly in construction and natural resources, carry a higher risk of fatal occupational injury than active-duty military service during many, though not all, periods of operation. While military service involves extreme hazards during wartime, high-risk civilian trades often have higher, more consistent fatality rates.   Suicide is a bigger threat to our soldiers than dying in the actual war.  

Higher Risk in Trades: Occupations like logging, fishing, roofing, and structural ironworking consistently have higher death rates per 100,000 workers than the U.S. armed forces.
Construction vs. Military: In some years, the construction industry has seen more total fatalities than the entire U.S. military.
Military Safety Statistics: In FY2021, the Army's on-duty ground fatality rate was 1.3 per 100,000 soldiers, which is lower than many high-risk civilian industries, with the majority of military deaths occurring off-duty, often in vehicle accidents.
Contextual Risk: The highest-risk trade (logging) has historically shown significantly higher fatality rates (e.g., 127.8 per 100,000 in 2012) compared to the U.S. military, even during the peak of conflicts in Iraq and Afghanistan.
Suicide Rates: While on-the-job fatalities are lower, active-duty military personnel face high risk due to suicide, with some studies showing post-9/11 suicides being four times higher than combat deaths

replies.author: @sicilianr1
replies.text:

Construction Trades (General): ~10 per 100,000
U.S. Army (On-duty, FY21): 1.3 per 100,000

replies.author: @lasourisaboyante
replies.text:
This is a much much bigger operation than the other two and it needs to happen. It has to happen before they get a nuclear weapon. Fortunately the Iran regime's response seems to be pushing every other Regional power to realize this is the moment in time to rid ourselves of this government once and for all.

To be clear: I oppose U.S. involvement in any new war where avoidable. Unfortunately, confrontation with Iran appears inevitable and necessary.

Ideally, the overthrow of Iran's regime should be led by a coalition of Israel, Saudi Arabia, UAE, and other Middle Eastern states—victimized by Iran's sponsorship of terrorism and insurgencies (Hezbollah, Hamas, Shia militias, Houthis). The U.S. role must be limited to decapitation strikes, destruction of nuclear facilities and military targets, leaflet drops over cities and bases ("The regime will fall—this is your chance for a better Iran. Rise up."), and regional security—without any U.S. troops on Iranian soil. The coalition remains on the ground to prevent resurgence while Iran transitions to a modern, democratic, secular republic.

The conflict has been inevitable since the 1979 Revolution and hostage crisis. A window after the Soviet Union's collapse—when Iran was weakest—was squandered by Clinton's dual-containment policy. Iran was a Soviet proxy; sponsorship weakened post-USSR, but revived as Russia regained power. Now, as Russia bleeds out in Ukraine, China is assuming that role. We cannot permit it. A nuclear Iran—60% enrichment stockpile ~441 kg (IAEA, Feb 2026), breakout time near zero—would be catastrophic. Action now is essential; delay invites nuclear disaster.

Presidents from Reagan to Trump to Biden bear blame for delay; current strikes (Feb 28, 2026) are late but necessary—better late than nuclear. We must act before China cements Iran's nuclear shield, rendering the task far more dangerous.
